1: τοῦ χειμάρρου τῶν Κέδρων — "the Kidron torrent/valley." Cheimarros (G5493) denotes a wadi — a seasonal streambed that runs with water in winter rains and dries in summer. The name Kedrōn derives from the Hebrew Qidrōn (darkness/black), associated with the brook David crossed when fleeing Absalom (2 Sam 15:23) — a detail likely not accidental to the Evangelist. κῆπος — "garden" — the Synoptics name it Gethsemane; John uses the garden detail, which will recur at the burial (19:41) and resurrection (20:15). The garden as the site of the arrest and the tomb as a garden signals an Edenic counternarrative: the second Adam entering the garden where the first Adam fell.
2: ᾔδει δὲ καὶ Ἰούδας ὁ παραδιδοὺς αὐτόν τὸν τόπον — "Now Judas, the one betraying him, also knew the place." The participle ho paradidous — "the one delivering over/betraying" — is present, characterizing Judas by his ongoing function, not merely the act. Paradidōmi (G3860) carries the double register of handing over (to authorities) and betrayal (of trust); the Evangelist uses it without softening. The explanation — Jesus had met there often with his disciples — underscores that the garden was a place of intimacy now turned into a trap by insider knowledge.
3: τὴν σπεῖραν — "the cohort/detachment." Speira (G4686) is the Greek term for a Roman military unit, normally a cohort of 480–600 soldiers; in practice "a detachment" here may indicate a maniple or smaller unit. The presence of Roman soldiers (alongside temple police and officials) from the outset in John's account is theologically significant: the arrest is under the joint authority of Roman empire and Jewish leadership — the powers of this world act together. μετὰ φανῶν καὶ λαμπάδων καὶ ὅπλων — "with torches and lanterns and weapons" — the detail is ironic: they come with artificial light to arrest the Light of the world, with weapons to arrest the one who will lay down his life voluntarily.
4: Ἰησοῦς οὖν εἰδὼς πάντα τὰ ἐρχόμενα ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν — "Jesus, therefore, knowing all the things coming upon him." Eidōs — perfect participle of oida (G1492), the verb of intuitive or complete knowing, not the relational ginōskō. The Evangelist stresses Jesus's sovereign foreknowledge: the arrest does not happen to a surprised victim but to one who foreknew it and chose it. ἐξῆλθεν — "went out" — Jesus takes the initiative, coming to meet the arresting party rather than waiting to be seized.
5: Ἰησοῦν τὸν Ναζωραῖον — "Jesus the Nazarene" — the geographical designation used at the arrest, the trial placard (19:19), and the post-resurrection announcement (20:15; cf. Acts 2:22). ἐγώ εἰμι — the absolute I-AM without predicate. In Greek, "I am he" would be egō eimi autos or simply egō; the bare egō eimi carries the register of the divine self-disclosure formula of Exod 3:14 LXX and the Isaianic "I AM" declarations (Isa 43:10 LXX: egō eimi). The Evangelist has used it at 8:24, 8:28, 8:58; here it is the operative term that causes the arrest party to fall back. The parenthetical note about Judas standing with them maximizes the irony: the betrayer is present when the divine name drops them to the ground.
6: ἀπῆλθον εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω καὶ ἔπεσαν χαμαί — "they went back and fell to the ground." Chamai (G5476, "to the ground") appears only here and at 9:6 (where Jesus spits on the ground to make clay); the rare word links the healing of the blind man and the arrest — divine power operates at ground level. The falling backward of the armed detachment at the utterance of egō eimi is a theophany response: comparison with the prostrations at divine appearances in the OT (Dan 8:17; Ezek 1:28; Rev 1:17) makes clear the Evangelist's intent. The arresting party cannot arrest Jesus; he gives himself over.
7: Τίνα ζητεῖτε — the question repeated verbatim from v. 4. The Evangelist's repetition is deliberate: after the display of divine authority in the I-AM and the falling backward, Jesus asks again the same question. The soldiers and officials must answer again — "Jesus the Nazarene" — and receive the same reply. The repetition is a literary device of patient sovereignty: Jesus controls the arrest scene even while submitting to it.
8: εἶπον ὑμῖν ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι — "I told you that I am he." The I-AM formula is now restated plainly. εἰ οὖν ἐμὲ ζητεῖτε, ἄφετε τούτους ὑπάγειν — "if then you seek me, let these go" — aphete (aorist imperative of aphiēmi, G863): release, let go. The Evangelist portrays Jesus as negotiating the terms of the arrest, securing the disciples' freedom as a condition — a detail unique to the Fourth Gospel. This connects to the "good shepherd" motif of ch. 10: the shepherd gives his life and the sheep go free.
9: ἵνα πληρωθῇ ὁ λόγος ὃν εἶπεν — "that the word he had spoken might be fulfilled." The Evangelist uses the standard fulfillment formula (hina plērōthē) but here applies it not to a scripture but to Jesus's own prior word (17:12: "I have not lost one of those you gave me"). Jesus's words have the same fulfillment-authority as Scripture — a significant claim that runs through the Johannine passion narrative.
10: Σίμων οὖν Πέτρος ἔχων μάχαιραν εἵλκυσεν αὐτήν — "Simon Peter therefore, having a sword, drew it." Machaira (G3162): a short sword or large knife, the standard personal sidearm. τοῦ ἀρχιερέως τὸν δοῦλον — "the high priest's servant." Only the Fourth Gospel names the servant: Μάλχος (G3124). The naming is a marker of concrete tradition — the Evangelist or his source knew who Malchus was. The specific ear named (tēn dexian, the right ear) adds further precision. ἀπέκοψεν αὐτοῦ τὸ ὠτάριον τὸ δεξιόν — "cut off his right ear" — ōtarion (G5621): diminutive of ous, perhaps "ear-lobe" rather than the whole ear.
11: Βάλε τὴν μάχαιραν εἰς τὴν θήκην — "Put your sword into the sheath." The command is a present-tense imperative (bale can also be aorist), but the gesture is unmistakable: the sword is sheathed. τὸ ποτήριον ὃ δέδωκέν μοι ὁ πατήρ, οὐ μὴ πίω αὐτό — "the cup that the Father has given me, shall I not drink it?" Potērion (G4221) — the cup is the Gethsemane language present in the Synoptics (Matt 26:39; Mark 14:36; Luke 22:42) but absent from John's Gethsemane-equivalent. The Evangelist introduces it here instead, as Jesus accepts the arrest: the cup metaphor for divinely-assigned suffering and death (cf. Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17; Jer 25:15). The double negative ou mē piō (strong negation with subjunctive) is a rhetorical question expecting the answer "of course I will drink it."
12: ἡ σπεῖρα καὶ ὁ χιλίαρχος καὶ οἱ ὑπηρέται τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "the cohort and the commander and the officers of the Jews" — three parties to the arrest: Roman soldiers, their commanding officer (chiliarchos, G5506, lit. "commander of a thousand," a tribune), and Jewish temple police. The binding of Jesus (ἔδησαν, aorist: they bound him) occurs immediately — the legal formality of custody asserted. Jesus goes bound through the rest of the trial narrative.
13: Ἄνναν πρῶτον — "to Annas first." The sequence — Annas before Caiaphas — is unique to John. Annas had been high priest from AD 6–15; Caiaphas (his son-in-law) held the office AD 18–36. Annas retained enormous political influence and is addressed or referred to as high priest by both Luke (Luke 3:2; Acts 4:6) and John. The appearance before Annas is a preliminary examination — the factual hearing before the formal proceeding at Caiaphas's house. ὁ πενθερὸς τοῦ Καϊάφα — "the father-in-law of Caiaphas" — a kinship detail explaining the first transfer.
14: ἦν δὲ Καϊάφας ὁ συμβουλεύσας τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις ὅτι συμφέρει ἕνα ἄνθρωπον ἀπολέσθαι ὑπὲρ τοῦ λαοῦ — "It was Caiaphas who had advised the Jews that it was expedient for one man to die for the people." The Evangelist reaches back to 11:50, where Caiaphas's pragmatic counsel was identified as inadvertent prophecy. Sympherō (G4851): "to be expedient/profitable" — the political calculus of one death for national preservation. The Evangelist quotes this without comment, allowing the irony to work: the man who authorized the arrest is the one whose political prophecy the trial is now fulfilling in ways he did not intend.
15: ὁ ἄλλος μαθητής — "the other disciple" — the Beloved Disciple, unnamed throughout the Gospel (13:23; 19:26; 20:2; 21:7,20). γνωστὸς τῷ ἀρχιερεῖ — "known to the high priest" — gnōstos (G1110): one who is known, a recognized acquaintance. How the Beloved Disciple had this connection is unexplained; the tradition is taken as known to the original readers. This connection allows him to enter the aulē (courtyard/palace) of the high priest — the inner residential compound, not a public space.
16: εἱστήκει δὲ ὁ Πέτρος πρὸς τῇ θύρᾳ ἔξω — "Peter was standing at the door outside" — pluperfect heistēkei: he had taken up his standing position. The door between outside and inside becomes the spatial emblem of Peter's ambiguous position — he wants to follow but cannot enter, exactly as Jesus told him (13:36–38). ἡ παιδίσκη ἡ θυρωρός — "the girl who kept the door" — a female doorkeeper. The first challenge to Peter comes from the least threatening social figure imaginable: a servant girl. The irony of the arrest scene's powerful men falling at egō eimi while Peter caves at a doorkeeper's question is structurally intentional.
17: μὴ καὶ σὺ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν εἶ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τούτου — "You aren't also one of the disciples of this man, are you?" The construction with mē signals a question expecting a negative answer — the girl herself frames it as unlikely. οὐκ εἰμί — "I am not" — Peter's first denial is a stark reversal of the I-AM formula Jesus used in vv. 5–8. Where Jesus's egō eimi (I am) was the divine self-disclosure that felled soldiers, Peter's ouk eimi (I am not) is the self-protective denial. The Evangelist's juxtaposition is deliberate and devastating.
18: ἀνθρακιάν — "a charcoal fire." Anthrakia (G439) appears only twice in the NT: here (18:18) and at 21:9, where the risen Jesus has prepared a charcoal fire for the disciples on the shore. The rare word is a Johannine echo-marker: Peter warms himself at a charcoal fire during the denial; the risen Christ will meet Peter at a charcoal fire for the restoration. The verbal connection between the two scenes is not accidental. ἦν δὲ ψῦχος — "it was cold" — one of the Gospel's vivid physical details; the night cold explains the fire and sets the atmospheric register of the trial scenes.
19: ὁ οὖν ἀρχιερεὺς ἠρώτησεν τὸν Ἰησοῦν περὶ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ καὶ περὶ τῆς διδαχῆς αὐτοῦ — "The high priest then questioned Jesus about his disciples and about his teaching." Annas's two questions — about followers (potential insurgent movement) and teaching (possible blasphemy or sedition) — are the standard preliminary categories of a Jewish judicial inquiry. Didachē (G1322): teaching, doctrine. The Evangelist does not identify this as a formal Sanhedrin proceeding — the formal judgment was Caiaphas's (11:50) and the legal death-verdict will be in 19:6–7. This is an interrogation.
20: ἐγὼ παρρησίᾳ λελάληκα τῷ κόσμῳ — "I have spoken openly to the world." Parrēsia (G3954) — literally "all-speech," frank and public disclosure — is the legal claim Jesus makes: nothing was said in private that is not on public record. Lelalēka — perfect, "I have spoken and this speaking stands." ἐν συναγωγῇ καὶ ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ — "in synagogue and in the temple" — the two primary public institutions of Jewish religious life; Jesus's teaching venues were the most scrutinized and crowded spaces available. ἐν κρυπτῷ ἐλάλησα οὐδέν — "in secret I spoke nothing" — the privacy charge implicit in Annas's questioning is denied outright. Jesus is not a clandestine teacher of a secret sect.
21: τί με ἐρωτᾷς; ἐρώτησον τοὺς ἀκηκοότας — "Why do you question me? Ask those who have heard me." Jesus's reply invokes the standard Jewish legal principle: testimony must come from witnesses (Deut 19:15), not from self-incrimination. Tous akēkootas — perfect participle: "those who have heard and still have that hearing." The witnesses exist; the court should call them. This is a legally astute response, not mere deflection: Jesus is pointing to the correct procedure under Torah.
22: ῥάπισμα ἔδωκεν — "gave a slap/blow." Rapisma (G4475): a blow with an open hand or rod — in rabbinic legal contexts, striking a party under judicial examination was a contempt of procedure if the examination had not yet concluded. Οὕτως ἀποκρίνῃ τῷ ἀρχιερεῖ — "Is this how you answer the high priest?" — the official's intervention treats Jesus's appeal to witnesses as impudence rather than legal procedure. The blow is an assertion of institutional power in the face of a challenge to its process.
23: εἰ κακῶς ἐλάλησα, μαρτύρησον περὶ τοῦ κακοῦ — "If I spoke wrongly, testify about the wrong." Kakōs (G2560): wrongly, badly — moral or factual wrongness. The conditional is first class (ei + aorist indicative), treating the condition as factually stated without assuming its truth: "if I spoke wrongly (which I did not) — prove it." εἰ δὲ καλῶς, τί με δέρεις — "but if rightly, why do you strike me?" Kalōs (G2573): rightly, well. Jesus' response to the blow is a demand for procedural justice: violence is not argumentation. The two conditionals — kakōs / kalōs — are clean legal logic rather than a refusal to cooperate.
24: ἀπέστειλεν οὖν αὐτὸν ὁ Ἄννας δεδεμένον πρὸς Καϊάφαν τὸν ἀρχιερέα — "Annas therefore sent him bound to Caiaphas the high priest." Dedemenon — perfect passive participle, "bound/having been bound" — the binding from v. 12 is still in effect. Annas transfers jurisdiction to the official high priest for the formal proceedings. The brevity of the Evangelist's account of both preliminary hearings (the Synoptics give more detail of the Caiaphas proceedings) reflects the Fourth Gospel's focus: the theological weight of the trial falls on the Pilate exchange, not on the Jewish court.
25: Μὴ καὶ σὺ ἐκ τῶν μαθητῶν αὐτοῦ εἶ — "You aren't also one of his disciples, are you?" The question form is identical to v. 17 — same mē construction anticipating denial — now from the servants and officials standing by the charcoal fire. Peter is asked to declare his allegiance in the very social group where belonging to Jesus would be most costly. ἠρνήσατο ἐκεῖνος καὶ εἶπεν· Οὐκ εἰμί — "he denied and said, I am not" — the same ouk eimi as v. 17. This is the second denial.
26: εἷς ἐκ τῶν δούλων τοῦ ἀρχιερέως, συγγενὴς ὢν οὗ ἀπέκοψεν Πέτρος τὸ ὠτίον — "one of the high priest's servants, being a relative of the one whose ear Peter cut off." The third challenge is the most specific: a kinsman of Malchus, who presumably recognized Peter from the garden. The question — "Didn't I see you in the garden with him?" — is virtually an accusation; it has the force of an eyewitness identification. Ouk egō se eidon — "Did I not see you?" — double negative with egō for emphasis. Peter has no credible denial left; the kinship connection makes a lie nearly impossible to sustain.
27: πάλιν οὖν ἠρνήσατο Πέτρος — "Peter denied again" — the third denial, narrated with minimal drama; the Evangelist's restraint is its own commentary. καὶ εὐθέως ἀλέκτωρ ἐφώνησεν — "and immediately a cock crowed." Eutheōs (G2112): immediately — the temporal marker confirms the fulfillment of Jesus's prediction (13:38: "before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times"). The cock crow is the structural endpoint of the Peter narrative in the passion; the Evangelist returns to the courtroom. The Johannine account of Peter's breakdown contains no weeping (contrast Matt 26:75; Luke 22:62) — Peter's restoration will come separately (21:15–19).
28: ἄγουσιν τὸν Ἰησοῦν ἀπὸ τοῦ Καϊάφα εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον — "they lead Jesus from Caiaphas to the praetorium." Praitōrion (G4232): the Roman governor's official residence and judgment hall in Jerusalem — either the Antonia Fortress or Herod's Palace (scholars dispute). The Greek term transliterates the Latin praetorium. αὐτοὶ δὲ οὐκ εἰσῆλθον εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον, ἵνα μὴ μιανθῶσιν — "they themselves did not enter the praetorium, so that they might not be defiled." The defilement concern is Passover purity: contact with a Gentile dwelling (understood as containing leaven and perhaps corpse-impurity) would render them unable to eat the Passover meal. The irony the Evangelist deploys is sharp — they guard their ritual purity while handing over an innocent man to death. Miainō (G3392): to stain, defile.
29: ἐξῆλθεν οὖν ὁ Πιλᾶτος ἔξω πρὸς αὐτούς — "Pilate therefore went outside to them." The spatial choreography of the trial — inside/outside the praetorium — is the Evangelist's structural device. Pilate must repeatedly cross the threshold because the accusers will not enter; the back-and-forth movements (vv. 29, 33, 38; 19:1, 4, 9, 13) dramatize the governor caught between Roman jurisdiction inside and Jewish pressure outside. Τίνα κατηγορίαν φέρετε κατὰ τοῦ ἀνθρώπου τούτου — "What accusation do you bring against this man?" — the standard Roman judicial opening: the accuser must state the charge.
30: εἰ μὴ ἦν οὗτος κακοποιός, οὐκ ἄν σοι παρεδώκαμεν αὐτόν — "If this man were not a criminal, we would not have handed him over to you." The construction is second-class conditional (past contrary to fact): "if he were not [which he is]." The Jewish leaders do not actually state a charge — they assert the verdict and ask Pilate to ratify it. Kakopoios (G2555): evil-doer, criminal — a vague accusation that avoids the specifics they know Pilate would need to adjudicate.
31: Λάβετε αὐτὸν ὑμεῖς καὶ κατὰ τὸν νόμον ὑμῶν κρίνατε αὐτόν — "Take him yourselves and judge him by your own law." Pilate's retort pushes the matter back: if the charge falls under Jewish law, Jewish courts should handle it. οὐκ ἔξεστιν ἡμῖν ἀποκτεῖναι οὐδένα — "it is not permitted for us to put anyone to death." Exestin (G1832): it is lawful, permitted — a legal claim about Roman provincial authority. Crucifixion (as opposed to stoning) was a Roman death-penalty; Rome had reserved capital punishment for itself in Judea (a debated but likely historical reality under the procuratorship). The Jewish leaders want crucifixion specifically, which only Pilate can authorize.
32: ἵνα ὁ λόγος τοῦ Ἰησοῦ πληρωθῇ ὃν εἶπεν σημαίνων ποίῳ θανάτῳ ἤμελλεν ἀποθνῄσκειν — "that the word of Jesus might be fulfilled, which he spoke, signifying by what death he was going to die." The fulfillment notice applies to Jesus's own word — specifically the hypsōthēnai (being lifted up) sayings (3:14; 8:28; 12:32–33). Sēmainō (G4591): to signal, indicate — the same verb used in Rev 1:1 for symbolic revelation. Crucifixion is the "lifted up" death; stoning (the Jewish alternative) is not. The Evangelist's fulfillment formula here confirms that divine necessity governs the specific mode of execution.
33: εἰσῆλθεν πάλιν εἰς τὸ πραιτώριον ὁ Πιλᾶτος καὶ ἐφώνησεν τὸν Ἰησοῦν — Pilate goes back inside and summons Jesus privately — the spatial movement marks the beginning of the interior hearing that will be the theological center of the trial. Σὺ εἶ ὁ βασιλεὺς τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "Are you the king of the Jews?" The question carries the political charge: a king of the Jews is a rival to Caesar and a threat to Roman order. Basileus (G935): king, ruler — in Pilate's mouth a political category; in Jesus's answer it will be redefined.
34: Ἀφ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ σὺ τοῦτο λέγεις ἢ ἄλλοι εἶπόν σοι περὶ ἐμοῦ — "Do you say this from yourself, or did others tell you about me?" Jesus's counter-question is not evasion; it is a juridical clarification. The distinction between Roman and Jewish categories of "king" is decisive: if Pilate is asking about a political insurrectionist (his own concern), the answer is no; if he is repeating the Jewish leaders' theological charge, the question needs reframing. Jesus forces Pilate to locate the question before answering it.
35: Μήτι ἐγὼ Ἰουδαῖός εἰμι — "I am not a Jew, am I?" — mēti with egō emphatic: a scornful rhetorical question. Pilate disavows any stake in Jewish messianic categories. τὸ ἔθνος τὸ σὸν καὶ οἱ ἀρχιερεῖς παρέδωκάν σε ἐμοί — "Your own nation and the chief priests handed you over to me" — Pilate names the accusers and distances himself from their charge while acknowledging his own position as the decision-maker they require. τί ἐποίησας — "What have you done?" — Pilate's actual question: what is the concrete act being charged?
36: ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου — "My kingdom is not from this world." The critical preposition: ek tou kosmou toutou — "out of / from this world" — marks origin and source, not location. Jesus is not claiming his kingdom does not operate within the world but that it does not derive from the world's power structures. εἰ ἐκ τοῦ κόσμου τούτου ἦν ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμή, οἱ ὑπηρέται οἱ ἐμοὶ ἠγωνίζοντο ἄν — "if my kingdom were of this world, my servants would be fighting" — second-class conditional: contrary to fact. The test is armed resistance: world-kingdoms defend themselves by force. His servants are not fighting. ἡ βασιλεία ἡ ἐμὴ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐντεῦθεν — "my kingdom is not from here" — enteuthen (from here) closes the definition: the kingdom's source is elsewhere, its operation is therefore by different means.
37: Οὐκοῦν βασιλεὺς εἶ σύ — "So you are a king!" — oukoun is an inferential particle: Pilate draws the conclusion from Jesus's own words. Jesus accepts the designation with a qualification: σὺ λέγεις ὅτι βασιλεύς εἰμι — "you say that I am a king" — neither full affirmation nor denial, but an acknowledgment that the word is Pilate's category and the reality it points to is Jesus's. ἐγὼ εἰς τοῦτο γεγέννημαι καὶ εἰς τοῦτο ἐλήλυθα εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "for this I have been born and for this I have come into the world" — perfect tenses: the birth and the coming are standing facts. The double purpose is truth-bearing: ἵνα μαρτυρήσω τῇ ἀληθείᾳ — "that I might witness to the truth." Martyreō (G3140): to witness, testify — the language of the law court now applied to Jesus's entire mission. πᾶς ὁ ὢν ἐκ τῆς ἀληθείας ἀκούει μου τῆς φωνῆς — "everyone who is of the truth hears my voice" — ek tēs alētheias: out of / deriving from truth — the same source-preposition as the kingdom in v. 36. Belonging to truth is an ontological status, not a moral achievement.
38: Τί ἐστιν ἀλήθεια — "What is truth?" Pilate's famous question has been read as philosophical skepticism, rhetorical dismissal, weary pragmatism, or genuine inquiry. The Evangelist gives no answer — Pilate exits without waiting for one. The question lands in the reader's ear as the supreme irony of the trial: the one who is "the truth" (14:6) stands before the man who asks "what is truth?" and receives no answer because none is sought. οὐδεμίαν εὑρίσκω ἐν αὐτῷ αἰτίαν — "I find no charge/fault in him." Aitia (G156): cause, accusation, legal ground — Pilate's formal verdict of innocence is delivered three times in John (18:38; 19:4; 19:6). The repetition is juridically and theologically deliberate.
39: ἔστιν δὲ συνήθεια ὑμῖν ἵνα ἕνα ἀπολύσω ὑμῖν ἐν τῷ πάσχα — "Now there is a custom for me to release one prisoner to you at the Passover." The Passover amnesty — the custom of releasing one prisoner at the feast — is attested in the Gospels (all four) but not in extra-biblical Jewish or Roman sources; scholars debate its historicity. The Evangelist's account uses it narratively to force the crowd into an explicit choice. βούλεσθε οὖν ἀπολύσω ὑμῖν τὸν βασιλέα τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "do you want me to release to you the king of the Jews?" — Pilate uses the royal title ironically and mockingly, but in doing so he names Jesus with the title that will go on the cross (19:19).
40: ἐκραύγασαν οὖν πάλιν λέγοντες· Μὴ τοῦτον ἀλλὰ τὸν Βαραββᾶν — "They shouted again saying, Not this one but Barabbas." Kraugazō (G2905): to cry out loudly — a crowd shout, not deliberation. ἦν δὲ ὁ Βαραββᾶς λῃστής — "Now Barabbas was a robber/bandit." Lēstēs (G3027) is the same word used in 10:1 for the one who enters the sheepfold as a thief rather than through the gate. The Evangelist's word choice is not accidental: the crowd releases a lēstēs and crucifies the gate-giver, the shepherd. The name Barabbas — bar (son of) + abba (father) — adds another layer: they choose the son of a human father over the Son of the Father.
